meta.name: Aderrig Help Centre Premium Seed
meta.notes: Full production help seed. Keeps all existing categories and items, with enhanced guidance for dashboard flow, elections, notices, reports, governance and operational expectations.

items.title: About & Team
items.summary: Help for understanding the purpose of the platform, roles and community structure.
items.content:
## About & Team
If you want to understand how the community structure works, this page explains the key roles and the purpose of the platform.
### What you will find
- Community background
- Team and role information
- General context for how the site supports residents

items.title: Dashboard Overview
items.summary: A practical resident guide to dashboard flow, dependencies, saving changes and using each section correctly.
items.content:
## Dashboard Overview
This is your main working area in the system. Most private-area tasks start here, so it is worth checking this page carefully when you log in.

### Recommended flow
To avoid mistakes, use the dashboard in this order:
1. Review your **Profile**
2. Confirm your **Household** information is correct
3. Update **Parking & Vehicles** if needed
4. Check **Notices** and **Garda & Safety** updates
5. Use **Reports** only when you need to record an incident

### Why this order matters
- Your household and profile details support validation across the platform
- Parking and vehicle records depend on the correct household/property record
- Notices and alerts may depend on your approved account and role
- Reports create a system reference and should be completed carefully

### What you can do here
- Review your resident profile details
- Manage permitted profile updates
- Review parking and vehicle information
- Submit election interest when an election is open
- Read resident notices and Garda/safety information
- Access reporting areas when you need to record an incident

### Important system behaviour
- Changes only apply after you click **Save**
- Some fields are locked to protect identity, address and record integrity
- If one section looks wrong, check whether it depends on your household/profile record first

### What to watch out for
- Do not leave a tab before saving changes
- Do not assume a report replaces external follow-up
- If a protected field is wrong, contact admin instead of trying to work around it
- If your account is pending approval, some sections may not be available yet

items.title: Elections & Expression of Interest
items.summary: Clear guidance for candidate interest, owner-only voting, valid votes and the 60% participation rule.
items.content:
## Elections & Expression of Interest
This section explains how election interest and voting work in the platform.

### Who can vote
Only verified **owners** are eligible to vote in community elections. This rule protects the integrity of decisions that relate to ownership responsibility.

### 60% participation rule
For an election result to be considered valid:
- at least **60% of eligible owners** must participate
- votes must be submitted within the election period
- votes must follow the rules configured for that election

If the 60% threshold is not reached, the result may be treated as invalid, paused for review, extended, repeated or handled according to the governance decision made by admin/owner oversight.

### What counts as a valid vote
A valid vote is one that:
- is submitted by an eligible owner
- is submitted only once for that eligible voting record
- is submitted during the open voting window
- follows the selection limits and instructions shown for that election

### Expression of interest
Before voting takes place, residents may be able to submit an expression of interest for an available role.
- The submission should explain the role you are interested in
- Your statement should be clear, relevant and professional
- Admin can review the submission before it is published as a candidate entry

### What happens next
- Admin reviews candidate interest
- Approved candidates may be shown for voting
- Eligible owners vote while the election is open
- The result is checked against the participation and validity rules

### What to watch out for
- Check that the election is currently open before submitting
- Candidate approval does not guarantee election success
- Voting rights are limited to eligible owners
- If participation is below 60%, the election result may not be valid

items.title: Household
items.summary: A clear guide to checking household information, understanding what can be updated and knowing how it affects other sections.
items.content:
## Household
If you need to review who belongs to your household or check the details linked to your address, this page will help you understand what is shown and what you can change.
### What you can do here
- Review household details
- Check linked members and core information
- Confirm whether anything needs to be corrected by admin
### How this connects to other sections
- Household details affect parking allocation and vehicle registration
- Household information may be used to validate access and communications
- If the core record is wrong, other linked sections may also appear wrong
### What happens next
If your household record is correct, related parts of the platform can use that data reliably. If something important cannot be edited by you, contact admin so the master record can be corrected.
### What to watch out for
- If something important is wrong and cannot be edited by you, contact admin
- Household details affect other areas such as parking and access

items.title: Parking & Vehicles
items.summary: Step-by-step help for adding a vehicle, declaring an empty space and understanding how parking details are validated.
items.content:
## Parking & Vehicles
If you need to register a vehicle or check your parking details, you are in the right place. This guide will help you complete the process without mistakes.
### What you can do here
- Add a new vehicle
- Review the vehicles already linked to your household
- Confirm whether the parking space is currently empty
### What you need before you start
- Your licence plate exactly as registered
- The vehicle type
- Basic vehicle details such as brand, model, colour and fuel type, if requested
### How to complete it
1. Open **Parking & Vehicles**
2. Read the parking policy first
3. Tick the confirmation boxes
4. Click **Add Vehicle**
5. Complete the form carefully
6. Save the registration
### What happens next
After saving, the vehicle registration or empty-space declaration becomes part of the property and household record and may be reviewed by admin where validation is required.
### How this connects to other sections
- Parking relies on the household and property record being correct
- Incorrect vehicle details can create issues for validation, resident records or enforcement
### What to watch out for
- Do not register visitor vehicles here
- Make sure the licence plate is correct before you save
- If you do not currently have a vehicle, use the empty-space declaration instead

items.title: Profile Management
items.summary: Help for checking your details, saving updates and understanding which fields are protected and why.
items.content:
## Profile Management
If you need to update your profile, this guide will help you understand which details you can change yourself and which fields are fixed for security or data integrity reasons.
### What you can do here
- Update your phone number
- Review your email
- Check your resident type
- Manage volunteer role information where available
- Upload a profile image if the page allows it
### What happens next
When you save permitted changes, the updated details can be used across your account experience. Protected fields stay locked so the system can preserve identity, validation and record quality.
### What to watch out for
- Your email is protected and cannot be edited here
- Save your changes before moving to another tab

items.title: Community Alerts
items.summary: Guidance for reading community alerts, understanding urgency and knowing when to act.
items.content:
## Community Alerts
Community Alerts are used for important security or community messages. They are more direct than general notices and may be targeted to specific audiences.

### What you can do here
- Read current or recent alerts
- Check urgency and audience
- Follow any instructions included in the message

### Alerts versus notices
- **Notices** are often informational and may be routine
- **Alerts** are used when information needs stronger attention or quicker awareness

### What you should do
- Read the full alert before acting
- Check whether the alert is informational or action-required
- Follow instructions only when they are clearly stated
- Keep your contact/profile details up to date so communications reach the correct audience

### What to watch out for
- Do not assume every alert means emergency action is required
- Do not forward sensitive information unless the message says it is appropriate
- If there is immediate danger or a crime in progress, use official emergency channels

items.title: Notices, Garda & Safety
items.summary: Help for understanding notices, Garda/safety updates, urgency and when action is required.
items.content:
## Notices, Garda & Safety
This area helps you read resident notices and safety information clearly, without confusing information-only updates with action-required messages.

### Types of information
**Informational notices**
- General resident updates
- Community information
- No immediate action required

**Action-required notices**
- Time-sensitive updates
- Safety-related instructions
- Requests to check, confirm or follow a specific instruction

**Garda & safety information**
- Safety awareness content
- Garda-related updates where available
- Practical information for residents to stay informed

### How to use this section
- Read the full message before acting
- Check whether the notice is informational or action-required
- Follow instructions only when the message clearly asks you to do something
- Check dates and relevance, especially for time-sensitive updates

### What happens next
Some notices are simply for awareness. Others may connect to alerts, reports, community actions or admin follow-up.

### What to watch out for
- Not every notice requires action
- Acting on partial information can create confusion
- If something is urgent or involves safety, follow the instructions given and use official channels where appropriate

items.title: Report an Incident
items.summary: Detailed guidance for recording incidents, understanding responsibility, evidence limits and follow-up expectations.
items.content:
## Report an Incident
Use this form when you need to record an incident in the system for reference and control purposes.

### Important responsibility note
The incident report is the responsibility of the person who submits it.
You are responsible for:
- providing accurate information
- keeping your own evidence and records
- making any external report required, including to Gardaí or another authority where appropriate

### What this form is for
Use it to record:
- safety concerns
- suspicious activity
- damage or issues in shared areas
- community-related incidents that should be documented

### What this form does not do
The platform does **not**:
- replace Garda reporting or any official reporting channel
- guarantee action, investigation or resolution
- operate as an evidence repository
- manage external cases or follow-up on your behalf

### What you should include
- What happened
- Where it happened
- When it happened
- Who or what was involved, if relevant
- Clear supporting details that help describe the incident

### Uploading photos and videos
If the form allows photos or videos:
- the upload may be accepted as part of the submission process
- the system may retain only the file name or reference
- the system does not act as long-term storage for uploaded evidence
- you must keep your own copies of photos, videos and other evidence

### What happens after submission
- The system records the report
- An incident number/reference is created
- The entry becomes part of the internal reporting history
- You can use the reference number when discussing the issue later

### Best practice
- Be factual and specific
- Do not exaggerate or guess
- Include time and location where possible
- Keep your own evidence safely
- Contact Gardaí or the relevant authority directly when required

items.title: My Reports Status
items.summary: A practical guide to checking that your incident was recorded in the system and using the incident number as a reference.
items.content:
## My Reports Status
This area helps you confirm that an incident report was recorded and find the reference created by the system.

### What you can check
- Whether your incident was recorded
- The incident number or reference
- Basic details linked to your submission

### What this status does and does not mean
A recorded report means the platform has captured the incident entry for reference. It does not mean:
- an investigation has started automatically
- Gardaí have been notified automatically
- uploaded evidence is being stored by the platform
- an external case has been opened

### What happens next
Use the incident number for your own records and for any follow-up conversation. If the incident requires official reporting, you remain responsible for making that report through the proper channel.

### Tip
Keep your own evidence, note the incident number and follow up externally where needed.

items.title: Community Projects
items.summary: A practical guide to browsing active projects, understanding project status and knowing what the page does and does not imply.
items.content:
## Community Projects
If you want to follow community work, this page helps you understand what each project is about and how to read its status.
### What you can do here
- Review active and planned projects
- Read the project purpose and latest updates
- Understand what stage a project has reached
### What happens next
Project pages help residents stay informed, but not every project page requires action from you. Some are there simply to provide visibility and context.
### What to watch out for
- Some projects are informational only
- Timelines may change as the work develops

items.title: Access Control
items.summary: Guidance for controlling which roles can open each page, tab or feature.
items.content:
## Access Control
If you are controlling permissions, use this section carefully. It decides who can see what across the site.
### What you should verify
- Public vs private access
- Role hierarchy
- Whether sub-tabs and features match the intended audience
### Caution
A small permission change can affect multiple user journeys, so review the impact before saving.

items.title: Notices & Alerts
items.summary: Operational guidance for publishing notices, targeting audiences and keeping messages clear.
items.content:
## Notices & Alerts
If you are creating or managing alerts, this guide helps you publish the right message to the right audience without causing confusion.
### What you are controlling
- Notice content
- Audience targeting
- Expiry dates
- Home page visibility
### What you should check
- The title is clear
- The message explains exactly what people need to know
- Expiry dates are correct
- Audience filters are appropriate

items.title: Projects Monitoring
items.summary: Operational guidance for managing project records, progress updates and recipient tracking.
items.content:
## Projects Monitoring
If you are maintaining project records, this area helps you keep updates organised and useful for both internal review and resident communication.
### What you are managing
- Project status
- Ownership and accountability
- Update history
- Recipient tracking where relevant
### What you should check
- The status reflects the actual project stage
- Key dates and responsibilities are current
- Public-facing updates remain clear and accurate

items.title: Dashboard Overview
items.summary: A full operational guide to the resident dashboard, including self-service areas, workflow dependencies and what admin should verify.
items.content:
## Dashboard Overview
This guide helps admin understand what residents see and how the dashboard connects to the rest of the system.

### What the dashboard controls
- Resident self-service profile updates
- Parking and vehicle declarations
- Election interest submissions
- Resident notices and Garda/safety information
- Incident recording for system reference

### Workflow dependencies to verify
- Profile and household details should support the private-area access model
- Household accuracy affects parking and validation
- Reports should create a system reference without implying evidence storage or external case handling
- Notices and alerts should match the current access-control rules

### What admin should check
- Resident-facing instructions are clear and current
- Locked fields remain protected
- The tab order still supports the real resident journey
- Content does not promise behaviour the live platform does not provide

### Operational note
When residents report confusion, check Dashboard guidance first. It is the main private-area entry point and sets expectations for the rest of the platform.

items.title: Elections Administration
items.summary: Guidance for interest review, candidate handling and voting workflow checks.
items.content:
## Elections Administration
Use this area carefully. Election wording and workflow must remain fair, transparent and consistent with the live rules.

### Core voting rule
Only verified **owners** are eligible to vote.

### 60% participation rule
For a result to be treated as valid, at least **60% of eligible owners** must participate. If the threshold is not met, the result may need to be reviewed, extended, repeated or treated as invalid according to governance decisions.

### What admin should review
- Whether the election is currently open
- Whether candidates were reviewed before publication
- Whether candidate visibility is correct
- Whether voting eligibility is limited to owners
- Whether participation reached the 60% threshold
- Whether vote records are complete and not duplicated

### Why this matters
Election content affects trust. Residents should clearly understand who can stand, who can vote, and why a result is valid or not valid.

### Good practice
Do not publish election results until voting eligibility, participation threshold and result consistency have been checked.

items.title: Handbook Management
items.summary: Guidance for importing, editing and publishing Help Center content from the admin side.
items.content:
## Handbook Management
If you are updating the Help Center, this is the area you will use. It is the back-office for the content that residents read in the Help Center.
### What you can do here
- Import the packaged help content
- Edit articles
- Adjust categories
- Publish updates
### Important rule
Residents do not manage content here. They only read what has already been published.
### Good practice
When you update wording, keep the resident language direct, clear and friendly.

items.title: Household Management
items.summary: Operational guidance for maintaining accurate household records, cross-module consistency and reliable downstream behaviour.
items.content:
## Household Management
If you are responsible for household data, this section helps you keep the core resident record accurate and aligned with the rest of the platform.
### Why it matters
- Household data influences parking allocation
- It can affect resident validation and access
- It supports accurate communication and issue resolution
### What you should check
- Member names and core details
- Address and Eircode consistency
- Links between the household record and related modules
### Workflow note
If the household record is wrong, errors can surface elsewhere. Fix the source record first so linked areas remain consistent.

items.title: Parking & Vehicles
items.summary: Full operational guidance for parking records, resident declarations, validation checks and downstream record integrity.
items.content:
## Parking & Vehicles
If you are managing parking records, this guide helps you understand both the resident workflow and the checks that protect the integrity of the data.
### What this area covers
- Parking space details linked to the residence
- Vehicle registrations submitted by residents
- Empty-space declarations
- Policy acceptance and owner confirmation
### What you should verify
- The resident has a valid parking allocation where required
- Visitor vehicles are not being entered in the owner / resident register
- Duplicate or incorrect registrations are corrected quickly
### Operational checks
- Confirm the parking policy link is current
- Confirm declarations match the property record
- Check that any warning notes shown on screen still reflect the active parking rules
### Workflow note
Parking records should stay aligned with the household record and any enforcement or validation process that depends on them.

items.title: Incident Reports
items.summary: Operational guidance for reviewing recorded incident entries, file-name references and incident numbering without implying evidence storage or case handling.
items.content:
## Incident Reports
This guide explains how to review incident submissions without overstating what the platform does.

### What the system records
- The incident details entered by the resident
- The incident number/reference created for control
- The fact that files may have been attached to the form
- File names or references where available

### What the system does not do
- It does not replace Garda reporting
- It does not operate as an evidence repository
- It does not manage external cases
- It does not guarantee investigation or resolution

### What admin should check
- The report has enough detail to identify the event
- Time and location are recorded where known
- The incident number/reference exists
- File references appear correctly where uploads were provided
- The resident-facing wording remains clear about responsibility

### File handling note
Residents remain responsible for keeping their own evidence. The platform entry should be treated as a reporting log, not as evidence storage.

### Recommended workflow
- Confirm the report was recorded correctly
- Use the incident number for internal reference
- Avoid implying that the platform has taken over external follow-up
- Direct residents to official channels where appropriate

items.title: Residents Management
items.summary: Complete guidance for reviewing resident registrations, approval workflow and record quality.
items.content:
## Residents Management
If you are reviewing resident registrations, this is one of the most important admin areas. It is where you confirm who has access and keep the resident register clean.
### What you can do here
- Review pending registrations
- Approve or reject residents
- Correct key resident details
- Maintain volunteer and coordination roles
### What you should check before approval
- The record is complete
- The address and Eircode are valid
- The person belongs to the correct household or property
### Why this matters
Resident approval affects access to private areas and can also influence notices, household data and role-based permissions.

items.title: Tasks Management
items.summary: Operational guidance for assigning tasks, tracking progress and keeping follow-up clear.
items.content:
## Tasks Management
If you are using tasks to organise follow-up work, this section helps you keep responsibilities visible and progress easy to track.
### What you should do
- Create tasks with clear titles
- Assign ownership where needed
- Keep status updates current
- Close tasks only when the work is truly complete

items.title: Tools & Maintenance
items.summary: Operational help for backups, exports, restore actions and diagnostics.
items.content:
## Tools & Maintenance
If you are using maintenance tools, this guide helps you avoid unnecessary risk.
### What you can do here
- Run backups
- Review restore options
- Export operational data
- Use diagnostics and maintenance utilities
### What to watch out for
- Always confirm what will be affected before a restore
- Keep export files secure because they may contain sensitive information

items.title: Alerts Governance
items.summary: Owner guidance for reviewing communication quality, audience control and whether alerts reflect real platform behaviour.
items.content:
## Alerts Governance
If you want to keep communications trustworthy, this section helps you review whether notices are accurate, timely and properly targeted.
### What you should monitor
- Clarity of wording
- Overuse of alerts
- Whether visibility rules are being applied correctly
### Governance note
Alerts should stay aligned with actual operational activity and should not imply action or urgency that the platform workflow does not support.

items.title: Projects Oversight
items.summary: Owner guidance for checking strategic alignment, delivery visibility and whether project reporting remains trustworthy.
items.content:
## Projects Oversight
If you need a higher-level view of community work, this owner guide helps you review project health and communication quality.
### What you should review
- Whether projects are progressing as expected
- Whether updates are transparent and useful
- Whether monitoring data supports sound decisions
### Governance note
Public-facing project updates should remain consistent with the internal operational record and should avoid overstating certainty where timelines may still move.

items.title: Dashboard Overview
items.summary: Use this view to compare the resident and admin guidance, validate the dashboard journey and check that cross-module logic is still clear.
items.content:
## Dashboard Overview
If you want to validate the quality of the dashboard guidance across the platform, this owner view gives you the wider picture.
### What you should review
- Whether the resident wording is clear and welcoming
- Whether the admin guidance includes the operational detail required
- Whether the page order still supports a smooth user journey
### Cross-role quality control
- The dashboard should explain how core areas connect without overwhelming the resident
- Incident reporting should be described as a system record with an incident number, not as evidence storage or case management
- Cross-module dependencies such as household, parking and reports should be reflected consistently across roles
### Quality control tip
Use the role tabs on this page to compare how the same area is described for Resident, Admin and Owner access.

items.title: Household Governance
items.summary: Owner guidance for reviewing household data quality, downstream impact and the reliability of connected sections.
items.content:
## Household Governance
If you want to keep the platform reliable, household accuracy must stay high. This owner view helps you see the wider importance of that data.
### What you should review
- Whether admin guidance is sufficient to keep records clean
- Whether resident-facing instructions avoid confusion
- Whether downstream modules are relying on stable household data
### Governance note
Household quality has a direct effect on parking, access and record confidence across the platform.

items.title: Parking & Vehicles
items.summary: Owner oversight for parking policy quality, enforcement consistency and whether resident guidance matches the live workflow.
items.content:
## Parking & Vehicles
If you need to review how parking is controlled across the platform, this owner view helps you check both resident clarity and administrative rigour.
### What you should monitor
- Whether residents are given clear instructions before they submit a registration
- Whether admin guidance properly covers validation and misuse prevention
- Whether the parking policy and declarations remain aligned with the live rules
### Governance note
This section is especially important because parking decisions can create disputes. Clear wording and strong validation reduce avoidable conflict.

items.title: Permissions & Publishing Control
items.summary: Owner guidance for access governance, publication checks and change control across sensitive areas of the platform.
items.content:
## Permissions & Publishing Control
If you are responsible for final oversight, this section helps you control who can access key areas and how Help Center content is published.
### What you should review
- Access rules for critical pages
- Who is allowed to edit help content
- Whether changes are being published in a controlled way

items.title: Platform Governance
items.summary: Owner guidance for reviewing content quality, platform consistency and whether published help matches real system behaviour.
items.content:
## Platform Governance
This owner guide helps keep the platform reliable, understandable and aligned with real system behaviour.

### What you should oversee
- Clarity of resident-facing guidance
- Completeness of admin operating guidance
- Consistency between Help Center content and live functionality
- Whether sensitive workflows are described accurately

### Critical areas to review
- Elections: owner-only voting and the 60% participation rule must be clear
- Reports: the platform records incidents but does not replace Garda reporting or store evidence as a case-management system
- Dashboard: residents should understand the recommended flow and dependencies
- Notices and alerts: messages should not create unnecessary alarm or imply unsupported action

### Recommendation
Use the role switcher in the Help Center to compare resident, admin and owner guidance for the same topic.

### Governance note
Help content should never promise behaviour the live platform does not support, including storage, visibility, voting eligibility, participation thresholds or external follow-up.

items.title: Quality Assurance Across Roles
items.summary: Owner guidance for checking how the same topic appears to Resident, Admin and Owner audiences and whether the messages stay aligned.
items.content:
## Quality Assurance Across Roles
If you want to compare how a topic is written for each role, this owner guide explains exactly what to look for.
### What you should compare
- Resident wording: clear, warm and easy to follow
- Admin wording: complete, operational and precise
- Owner wording: strategic, controlled and governance-focused
### Review tip
If the three views do not align, update the Handbook before it causes confusion in production.

items.title: Incident Oversight
items.summary: Owner guidance for monitoring incident-record quality, user expectations and whether the platform description matches the real system behaviour.
items.content:
## Incident Oversight
This owner guide helps you review incident-record quality and ensure the system description remains accurate.

### What you should monitor
- Whether residents understand that the report is their responsibility
- Whether the system is described as recording the incident, not investigating it automatically
- Whether incident numbering is clear and consistent
- Whether file-handling expectations are explicit
- Whether the Help Center avoids implying evidence retention or Garda case handling

### What good guidance should say
- The resident must provide accurate information
- The resident must keep their own evidence
- The platform creates an incident reference
- Official reporting remains outside the platform unless separately handled through the proper authority

### Governance note
The incident area should reduce uncertainty, not create false expectations. It should clearly state what the system records and what the resident remains responsible for.
